root_causes.cause: R2 does not have a route for the source network 192.168.0.0/24, so replies for traffic sourced from 192.168.0.12 are forwarded to R2's default next-hop (R3) instead of back to R1; this prevents ICMP replies from reaching the source and explains 0/25 pings succeeding.
evidence: R2 routing config shows only a default route: { "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "mask": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [ { "fwd": "10.0.0.6" } ] }
R1 has an explicit route for the destination network pointing at R2: { "prefix": "172.16.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "fwd-list": [ { "fwd": "10.0.0.2" } ] }
Interface addresses confirming source/destination placement: R1 GigabitEthernet0/0/0 = 192.168.0.1/24; R2 GigabitEthernet0/0/0 = 172.16.0.1/24

Interface addresses confirming source/destination placement: R1 GigabitEthernet0/0/0 = 192.168.0.1/24; R2 GigabitEthernet0/0/0 = 172.16.0.1/24

data.intent_description: R2 lacks a return route for source network 192.168.0.0/24, causing 0/25 pings from 192.168.0.12 to 172.16.0.12 to fail; after remediation ICMP replies should return to 192.168.0.12 and pings should succeed.

plan.problem: R2 has only a default route and no route for 192.168.0.0/24, so replies from 172.16.0.12 are not forwarded back to 192.168.0.12.
plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 pointing to next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R1) to restore the return path. Risk: brief routing change on R2; verify next-hop reachability.

parsed.intent_description: R2 lacks a return route for source network 192.168.0.0/24, causing 0/25 pings from 192.168.0.12 to 172.16.0.12 to fail; after remediation ICMP replies should return to 192.168.0.12 and pings should succeed.

evidence: Latest get_running_config for router2 (R2) shows routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list contains only the default route: { "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "mask": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [ { "fwd": "10.0.0.6" } ] }.
The running-config for R2 includes Serial0/1/0 = 10.0.0.2/30 (link to R1), confirming R1 is reachable as next-hop 10.0.0.2 if a route were configured.
No entry for 192.168.0.0/24 appears in the R2 routing section of the provided config; therefore the planned static route (192.168.0.0/24 -> 10.0.0.2) is not present.
remaining_issues: R2 does not have a route for 192.168.0.0/24. Replies sourced to 192.168.0.12 will not be returned via R1 until a route is added.
Planned fix (add static route on R2: 192.168.0.0/24 next-hop 10.0.0.2) has not been applied — re-run get_running_config after applying the route to verify resolution.

root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for the 192.168.0.0/24 source network, so it cannot forward ICMP replies back to 192.168.0.12 via R1.
evidence: R2 routing: "ip-route-interface-forwarding-list": [ { "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "mask": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [ { "fwd": "10.0.0.6" } ] } ]  (no 192.168.0.0/24 route present)
R1 interface: "GigabitEthernet0/0/0 has IP 192.168.0.1/24"
R2 interface: "GigabitEthernet0/0/0 has IP 172.16.0.1/24"

root_causes.type: Wrong egress path due to default route
root_causes.cause: Because R2 lacks a specific route to 192.168.0.0/24, R2 uses its default next-hop (10.0.0.6 -> R3) to reach that destination, sending replies away from R1 instead of back toward the 192.168.0.0/24 network.
evidence: R2 routing default: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd": "10.0.0.6"
R3 routing default: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [ { "fwd": "Loopback1" } ]  (indicates traffic from R2 will be forwarded toward R3/Loopback rather than to R1)
Topology: R1 <-> R2 link addresses 10.0.0.1/30 and 10.0.0.2/30 (path between R1 and R2 exists but R2 will not use it for 192.168.0.0/24 without a route)

root_causes.cause: An earlier attempt to add a static route to R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 failed (so the missing route remains).
evidence: previous_changes add_static_route result: "status": "error", "message": "Client error '400 Bad Request' ..." for adding route on router2 for network 192.168.0.0

root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for the 192.168.0.0/24 source network, so it cannot forward ICMP replies back to 192.168.0.12 via R1.

R1 interface: "GigabitEthernet0/0/0 has IP 192.168.0.1/24"
R2 interface: "GigabitEthernet0/0/0 has IP 172.16.0.1/24"

root_causes.type: Wrong egress path due to default route
root_causes.cause: Because R2 lacks a specific route to 192.168.0.0/24, R2 uses its default next-hop (10.0.0.6 -> R3) to reach that destination, sending replies away from R1 instead of back toward the 192.168.0.0/24 network.
evidence: R2 routing default: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd": "10.0.0.6"
R3 routing default: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [ { "fwd": "Loopback1" } ]  (indicates traffic from R2 will be forwarded toward R3/Loopback rather than to R1)
Topology: R1 <-> R2 link addresses 10.0.0.1/30 and 10.0.0.2/30 (path between R1 and R2 exists but R2 will not use it for 192.168.0.0/24 without a route)

messages.content:
Observed: ICMP from 192.168.0.12 to 172.16.0.12 was failing because R2 had no route back to 192.168.0.0/24 and was sending replies via its default next-hop (toward R3).

Diagnosis: Root cause was a missing/static route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 so return traffic did not go to R1.

Changes made: Added a static route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 (R1). Note: an earlier attempt with next-hop 10.0.0.2 failed; the subsequent change succeeded.

Verification: Passed — R2 running-config shows 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 and ping tests from R2 to 192.168.0.12 report 5/5 success. No remaining issues.

data.last_message:
Observed: ICMP from 192.168.0.12 to 172.16.0.12 was failing because R2 had no route back to 192.168.0.0/24 and was sending replies via its default next-hop (toward R3).

Diagnosis: Root cause was a missing/static route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 so return traffic did not go to R1.

Changes made: Added a static route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 (R1). Note: an earlier attempt with next-hop 10.0.0.2 failed; the subsequent change succeeded.

Verification: Passed — R2 running-config shows 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 and ping tests from R2 to 192.168.0.12 report 5/5 success. No remaining issues.
